stem: The values in a data set have mean \( [[m]] \). Each value \( x \) is replaced by \( y = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \). What is the mean of the new data set of \( y \)-values?
solution: A linear transformation \( y = ax + b \) shifts and scales the mean the same way: \( \bar{y} = [[a]]\cdot[[m]] + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This scales the mean by \( [[a]] \) but forgets to add the constant \( [[b]] \).
This adds \( [[b]] \) but forgets to multiply the mean by \( [[a]] \).
This subtracts \( [[b]] \) instead of adding it (a sign error).
This multiplies the added constant by \( [[a]] \) as well; only the original values are scaled.

stem: The values in a data set have standard deviation \( [[sigma]] \). Each value \( x \) is replaced by \( y = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \). What is the standard deviation of the new data set of \( y \)-values?
solution: A linear transformation scales the standard deviation by the size of the slope: \( \sigma_y = |[[a]]|\cdot[[sigma]] = [[answer]] \); shifting by \( [[b]] \) does not change the spread at all.
distractor_rationales: This keeps the sign of \( [[a]] \); a standard deviation must scale by \( |a| \), never come out negative.
This mistakenly adds the shift \( [[b]] \), but shifting a data set never changes its spread.
This squares the scale factor instead of taking its absolute value.
This assumes the standard deviation is unaffected by the scaling factor \( [[a]] \) at all.
This is off by one from the correct standard deviation.

stem: The average daily temperature over a week was \( [[meanC]]^\circ\text{C} \). Using the conversion \( F = \dfrac{9}{5}C + 32 \), what was the average daily temperature that week in degrees Fahrenheit?
solution: The mean transforms the same way the values do: \( \bar{F} = \dfrac{9}{5}\cdot[[meanC]] + 32 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds 32 but forgets to scale the Celsius mean by \( 9/5 \) first.
This scales by \( 9/5 \) but forgets to add the 32-degree offset.
This uses the inverse slope \( 5/9 \) (for converting Fahrenheit to Celsius) instead of \( 9/5 \).
This adds 32 to the Celsius mean before scaling, instead of after.
This is off by one from the correct Fahrenheit mean.

stem: A data set has mean \( [[m]] \). Each value \( x \) is transformed to \( y = [[a]]x + b \) for some constant \( b \). For what value of \( b \) does the new data set of \( y \)-values have mean \( 0 \)?
solution: The new mean is \( [[a]]\cdot[[m]] + b \); setting this equal to \( 0 \) and solving gives \( b = -[[a]]\cdot[[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign; it is the negative of the needed shift.
This divides instead of multiplying, and does not solve the mean equation at all.
This divides instead of multiplying (with a sign flip), and does not solve the mean equation at all.
This adds \( a \) and \( m \) together instead of solving \( am + b = 0 \) for \( b \).
This is off by one from the correct shift.
